coredata.dc:title: Tetraphenylethylene and N-isopropylacrylamide block polymer-grafted carbon dots with their application in cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, S.
Liao, B.
Yi, S.
Liang, E.
He, B.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) grafted with block polymer of tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and N-isopropylacrylamide (CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM)) were synthesized, which are aggregation-induced emission (AIE) nanoparticles. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) exhibited different fluorescence behaviors in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and water. In THF, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) could fluoresce, but only from CDs, and the TPE block showed no fluorescence. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched ,and the TPE block showed AIE, when the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were dispersed in water. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) showed no cytotoxicity, which could be easily internalized by human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells with high fluorescence, and they can be used as fluorescent tracers for living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Tetraphenylethylene (TPE)
Aggregation-induced effect (AIE)
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD chain transfer agents (CD-DATC)
Preparation of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the CDs (a) and CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) (b).
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a) and CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) (b) in THF excited at different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a) and CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) (b) dispersed in water at different excitation wavelength.
Schematic representation of the structure of the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in THF and water.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) in THF/water mixed solvent with different water contents excited at 320 nm, (b) photographs of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water/THF system with different water contents under UV light (365 nm).
Fluorescent emission spectra of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) film excited at different excitation wavelength.

The cell viabilities of human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) after 48 h of treatment with different concentrations of CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) determined by MTT (a), the fluorescence microscope images of human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) (b) and human embryonic kidney cells (HEK-293) (c) after cultured with CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) (250 μg/mL) for 2 h, respectively.

head.ce:title: Tetraphenylethylene and N-isopropylacrylamide block polymer-grafted carbon dots with their application in cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials for New Energy Storage and Conversion, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) grafted with block polymer of tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and N-isopropylacrylamide (CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM)) were synthesized, which are aggregation-induced emission (AIE) nanoparticles. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) exhibited different fluorescence behaviors in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and water. In THF, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) could fluoresce, but only from CDs, and the TPE block showed no fluorescence. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched ,and the TPE block showed AIE, when the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were dispersed in water. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) showed no cytotoxicity, which could be easily internalized by human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells with high fluorescence, and they can be used as fluorescent tracers for living cells.

Tetraphenylethylene and N-isopropylacrylamide block polymer-grafted carbon dots are aggregation-induced emission (AIE) nanoparticles, which can be easily internalized by human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells without cytotoxicity, and they can be used as fluorescent tracers for living cells.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials, are widely reported for their advantages of superior fluorescence, mass production [1], excellent biocompatibility [2–6], low toxicity, and good optical stability [7–10]. In this way, CDs also have been applied extensively in sensing [11,12] and bioimaging [13–15] etc. To improve the application value of CDs, CDs usually have been modified with other small molecules or polymers for endowing them new properties [16–19]. However, most of the reported functionalized CDs usually demonstrate only a single application. CDs that can achieve two or more functions have rarely been reported so far.

Tang et al. discovered a new kind of materials, which are tetraphenylethenes (TPEs) [20–25] with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties [26–30]. TPEs have been widely reported because of their high possible application especially in biomedicine fields [31–33]. Unfortunately, TPEs cannot fluoresce in solutions because of molecular rotating [34] and are usually hydrophobic, which indicate that it is impossible to directly apply TPEs in biomedical fields. Therefore, many hydrophilic AIE organic nanoparticles based on TPEs have been developed for different applications [35–39]. However, surfactants are usually used in constructing these AIE organic nanoparticles, and these surfactants cannot easily be removed, which limits their applications especially in biomedical field [40–42]. Developing other TPE-based AIE nanoparticles without surfactants will be beneficial for the application of TPEs.

Herein, we report such AIE nanoparticles based on tetraphenylethene (TPE), which are CDs grafted with block polymer of TPE and N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) (CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM)). The as-prepared CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) shows good dispersity in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and water, demonstrating hydrophilic AIE nanoparticles prepared by modifying CDs are successful. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the AIE nanoparticles could respond to the change of solvent. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) showed different fluorescence behaviors when they were in THF and water. In THF, the grafted block polymer showed no fluorescence, and the fluorescence emission of the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) was depended on excitation wavelength, which was just from the CDs. However, when the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were dispersed in water, they could form the structure similar to micelle, and then the polymer of TPE block exhibited AIE; they could fluoresce highly. The strong fluorescence properties of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) in water could make it hopefully as a multifunctional platform both in anticounterfeiting and biomedical areas. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) have good forming membrane performance, and the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) solid film also showed fluorescence properties. When sprayed with water, the fluorescence from the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) solid film could enhance. This performance makes CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) show promising application in the field of anticounterfeiting. Moreover, because of the good hydrophobicity, CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) could be well internalized by cells and showed no cytotoxicity. In living cells, it was very easy to detect the fluorescence from the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM), which can be potentially applied in biological field. This work not only can expand the scope of application of CDs but also offer some useful strategies for the design of novel multifunctional CDs.

The instruments used were as follows: Bruker-500MHz NMR Spectrometer, Bruker, Switzerland; American Perkin-Elmer FTIR-2000 infrared spectrum analyzer; F7000 fluorescence spectrometer Japan HITACHI; Parker Elmer Lambda25 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer; enzyme standard instrument BioTek Synergy H4, USA; inverted fluorescence microscope YKY-800, Germany; and Hitachi-7650 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Japan. Laser particle sizer was Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)pheno was obtained from Zhengzhou Acme. The S-1-Dodecyl-S’-(α, α′-dimethyl-α”-acetic acid) trithiocarbonate (DATC), N,N 0-dicyclohexylcarboiimide (DCC), and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (4-DMAP) were purchased from Aladin. THF, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), ethanol, 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Merck, and they were dewater. The Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. The water used was distilled water.

The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method as follows: placed 2.24 g EDTA.2Na to a hydrothermal reactor with 20 mL water and sealed, then heated to 220 °C and kept for 48 h. When the reaction was finished, waited for the reactor to cool down to 25 °C. Then rotated the obtained solution by vacuum rotary evaporation to get brownish black solid. And the brownish black solid was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol. Then filtered out the deposits and repeated the step thrice. The upper yellow solution containing CDs showed high blue-green luminescence under the UV light at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD chain transfer agents (CD-DATC)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis route of CD-DATC is shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). Dissolved CDs (0.100 g), DCC (0.310 g, 1.5 mmol), and 4-DMAP (0.0305 g, 0.25 mmol) in CH2Cl2 charged into a flask equipped with a magnetic stir bar. Put 0.910 g DATC on the solution with stirring at 25 °C for 7 days. Then the reaction mixture was filtered, and the solventCH2Cl2 was removed by vacuum rotary evaporation and obtained brownish-yellow powder. Dissolved the powders with ethanol and dialyzed the solution with ethanol (molecular weight cut off was 500) and repoured ethanol every 8 h for 2 days, finally obtained the CD-DATC solution.

Added 3.48 g 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)pheno, 1 g acryloyl chloride, and 0.1 mL triethylamine in a dried flask with a magnetic stir bar and dissolved by CH2Cl2. Then placed the flask in magnetic stirring apparatus at 25 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, put the solution in acetone, filtered the mixture, and repeated this step for three times. Finally got the yellow-brown solid, and it was acryloyloxy-TPE.

The synthesis route was shown in Fig. S2. Placed 10 mg CD-DATC, 5 mg acryloyloxy-TPE, and 0.1 mg AIBN in a dried flask and dissolved with THF. Then the flask was evacuated and then backfilled with nitrogen, repeated this step for three times under the condition of freezing and then sealed the flask by melting the glass. Kept the reaction at 70 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, precipitated the product with 10 mL of petroleum ether, finally obtained the CD-g-poly(TPE) by filtration and dried.

The synthesis route was shown in Fig. S3. Placed 10 mg CD-g-poly(TPE), 5 mg NIPAM, and 0.1 mg AIBN dissolved in THF to a dried flask. Under the condition of freezing, then evacuated the flask, and backfilled with nitrogen. Repeated this step three times. Finally sealed the flask by melting the glass. Then heated at 70 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, precipitated the product with petroleum ether, finally obtained the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) by filtration and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water
ce:section.ce:para: Added 5 mg CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) and magnet into a beaker. Then added 10 mL THF and put the beaker on a magnetic rotary stirrer and added water dropwise until the solution becomes clarified. And the solution was dialyzed with water (molecular weight cutoff was 3,000), and the water was repoured every 8 h for 2 days. Finally, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water was obtained.

Prepared the culture medium including DMEM and 10% fetal bovine serum. Then added human breast cancer cells or human embryonic kidney cells to the culture medium at the petri dishes and put the petri dishes at 37 °C and 5% carbon dioxide atmosphere.

Inoculated human breast cancer cells into 96-well plates at a density of 50,000 cells per well and nurtured for 24 h. Treated the cells with different concentrations of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) solution for 48 h. Then washed the cells with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and added MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 100 μg/mL) to the cells in each well. Added DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals formed by living cells after the MTT was removed. The absorbance was measured at 489 nm using a microplate analyzer.

Human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells were inoculated in culture dishes for 24 h. The media were removed, and a mixture of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water (500 μg/mL, 1,500 μL) and DMEM (1,500 μL) was added into the culture dishes and incubated for 2 h. CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) and DMEM were removed, and the culture dishes were washed by PBS solution for three times. The imaging of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) in cells was observed using Zeiss inverted fluorescence microscope with green light channels.

The morphology of the CDs and synthesized CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were characterized with TEM, and the results are given in Fig. 1 . Compared the morphology of CDs with that of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM), we can find that the mean size of the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) (3.51 nm) is obviously bigger than that of CDs (1.73 nm). It indicates that the block polymers of TPE and NIPAM should have been grafted on the surface of CDs.

The 1H NMR spectrum of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) in CDCl is given in Fig. S4. The signals located at δ = 6.92–7.15 ppm are attributed to the protons of the benzene groups of TPE block. The protons signals of methyl and methylene from the NIPAM block can be seen at δ = 0.98–1.31 ppm and 3.90–4.15 ppm, respectively. While the peaks at δ = 1.52–2.20 ppm and 2.11–2.31 ppm should be from the protons signals on the main chain of the block polymer. Compared with the 1H NMR results of CDs and CD-DATC we ever reported [16,17], we can again conclude that the synthesis of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) is successful.

The fluorescence emission spectra from CDs and CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) in THF are given in Fig. 2 . The emission properties of CDs show excitation dependence, as shown in Fig. 2a, whose emission wavelengths vary with excitation wavelengths. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) also shows one emission with excitation dependence in Fig. 2b. It means that only CDs fluoresce, in the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM), whereas the polymer of TPE does not fluoresce when in THF. Compared the emission of CDs with that of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) in THF, when excited at the same wavelength, we can find that the emission peaks of the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) have red shift. It also indicates that the grafted block polymer possibly influences fluorescence properties of CDs.

As is known, TPE can aggregate and show AIE when they are dispersed in non-solvent. When the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were dispersed in water, the internal TPE block will aggregate, whereas the external PNIPAM block will be dissolved in water. Then the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) will form a kind of structure similar to micelle. We investigated the fluorescence emission properties of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in water excited at 300–440 nm are given in Fig. 3 . The emission properties of CDs dispersed in water are similar to their properties in THF, also have excitation dependence. However, the emission spectra of the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) are different from that of CDs, and there is a fixed emission peak located at 460 nm in the spectra of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM), with varying excitation wavelength, and the emission intensity changes. The emissions with fixed emission peak should be from the TPE block in the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM). It indicated that the TPE block on the surface of CDs aggregated and showed AIE. That is to say, as represented in Fig. 4 , the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) indeed formed such kind of structure similar to micelle when dispersed in water. Meanwhile, as given in Fig. S5, the smaller mean hydrodynamics size (134.7 nm) in water than that in THF (189.3 nm) also proved that the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) formed micelle due to the aggregation of TPE block.

To reveal the relation between the forming process of micelle and the fluorescence properties of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM), we studied the fluorescence emission of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in the mixed solvent of THF and water with different volume proportion. As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, when the volume ratio of THF/water is 9:1, the emission peak locates 412 nm, which is from CDs in the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM). With the increase of the volume proportion of water, the emission peak starts to shift red, and the emission intensity decreases. When the water volume proportion is 90% in the mixture, the emission peak shifts to 460 nm, and the fluorescence from CDs disappears. It indicates that the TPE block aggregated and micelle formed, then show AIE in the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM). When the water volume proportion increases to 100%, the fluorescence emission intensity increases almost two times, which shows that the block TPE aggregates more closely and AIE enhances. With the observation under UV light, the change of fluorescence emission of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) dispersed in the mixture of THF and water with the increase of water volume proportion was proved again. As shown in Fig. 5b, when the water volume proportion is 10%, CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) fluoresce weakly blue. However, the fluorescence intensity from CDs decreases due to quenching when the volume ratio of water/THF is 2:8. With the gradual increase of water proportion, the blue fluorescence gradually enhances. As TPE is a kind of hydrophobic AIE molecule, in pure THF solution, the TPE block on the CDs are non-aggregated. At this moment, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) show the fluorescence of CDs. Then, with the increase of the water content in the THF/water solvent system, the TPE block gradually collapses on the CDs surface. The aggregation degree of TPE block gradually increases with increasing water content. As the aggregation degree increases, the fluorescence of CDs gradually is quenched, and the AIE effect of TPE increases. That is to say, when the content of water in the THF/water system is low, the aggregation degree of the TPE block is not enough, the TPE block can hardly fluoresce, but it can quench part of the fluorescence of CDs. In high water content, the aggregation degree of the TPE block is more high, which causes the fluorescence from CDs is completely quenched, whereas the TPE block shows strong AIE, when the water content is 100%.

Because the CDs have been grafted with the block polymer of TPE and NIPAM, and the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) have good processability, they could be easily processed into film or fiber. The fluorescence emission properties of the film from the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) were studied as shown in Fig. 6 . When excited at the range of 300–420 nm, only one emission can be seen, and the peaks always locate at 465 nm. It indicates that the fluorescence of CDs was quenched in the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) solid film, and the fluorescence was just from the aggregated TPE block. Furthermore, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) film can fluoresce weak blue under UV light, as given in Fig. S6, the fluorescence can be enhanced when the film sprayed with water. It means that the TPE block aggregated closely when sprayed with water in the film, and the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) can be potential as anticounterfeiting and encryption material.

For biomedical applications, we investigated the cytotoxicity of the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) in human breast cancer cells with the MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 7 a, we treated the human breast cancer cells with different concentrations of CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) for 48 h. Compared with the blank, the cell concentrations all were far high when treated with the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM). The results showed that the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) not only had no cytotoxicity but also could promote cells proliferation. As shown in the Fig. 7b,c, the blue fluorescence from the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM) could be easily detected in human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells after 2 h culture, respectively. The result indicates satisfactory in vitro biocompatibilities at all dosages of the CD-g-poly (TPE-block-NIPAM), enabling them to serve as a potential probe for living cell imaging.

In summary, we successfully obtained the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) AIE nanoparticles with good biocompatibility and fluorescence performance. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) could form such structure similar to micelle dispersed in water and show AIE. Meanwhile, the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) show good processability, can be potential as anticounterfeiting and encryption material. The CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM) showed no cytotoxicity and could be easily internalized by human breast cancer cells and human embryonic kidney cells, and they can be used as fluorescent tracers for living cells.

B.L. and B.H. conceived the idea and designed the experiments. S.Y. conducted the experiments with assistance from S.Y. S.Y. synthesized and characterized the CD-g-poly(TPE-block-NIPAM). E.L. synthesized acroyloyloxy-TPE. S.Y., B.L., S.Y., and E.L. contributed to analysis and discussion of the results. S.Y., B.L., and B.H. co-wrote the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project Nos. 21776218 and 52173279), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Project No. 2020JJ4303), and Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (20A181).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2021.100703.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high-yield and versatile method for the synthesis of carbon dots for bioimaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.-P Li, C.-X. Lu, S.-J. Li, A high-yield and versatile method for the synthesis of carbon dots for bioimaging applications, J. Mater. Chem. B. 5 (2017) 1935-1942 https://doi.org/10.1039/C6TB03003C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cyto-toxity biocompatibility and cellular response of carbon dots-plasmonic based nano-hybrids for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.N. Emam, Samah A, Loutfy, A.A. Mostafa, H. Awad, M.B. Mohamed, Cyto-toxity biocompatibility and cellular response of carbon dots-plasmonic based nano-hybrids for bioimaging, RSC Adv.. 7 (2017) 23502-23514 https://doi.org/10.1039/C7RA01423F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-releasing hydrogels with antibacterial activity, high biocompatibility, and fluorescence performance as candidate materials for wound healing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.-C. Cui, J.-D. Sun, J. Ji, X.-X. Yang, K.-M. Wei, H.-W. Xu, Q.-Y. Gu, Y.-Z. Zhang, X.-L. Sun, Carbon dots-releasing hydrogels with antibacterial activity, high biocompatibility, and fluorescence performance as candidate materials for wound healing, J. Hazard Mater.. 3 (2021) 124330 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.124330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocopatibility and bioimaging potential of fruit-based carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dias, N. Vasimalai, M. P. Sarria, I. Pinheiro, V. Vilas-Boas, J. Peixoto, B Espina, Biocopatibility and bioimaging potential of fruit-based carbon dots, ACS Nano. 9 (2019) 199 https://doi.org/10.3390/nano9020199.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of poly (4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) and its conjugated copolymers initiated by easily accessible carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Maruthapandia, V.B. Kumara, M. Levine, A. Gedankena, Fabrication of poly (4,4′-oxybisbenzenamine) and its conjugated copolymers initiated by easily accessible carbon dots, Eur. Polym. J. 109 (2018) 153-161 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2018.09.050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ generation of carbon dots within a polymer matrix

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Fernandes, K.A. Heslop, A. Kelarakis, M.J. Krysmann, L. Estevez, In situ generation of carbon dots within a polymer matrix, Polymer. 188 (2020) 122159. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2020.122159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots and stability of their optical properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Javed, D.M. O'Carroll, Carbon dots and stability of their optical properties, Part. Part. Syst. Char. 38 (2021) 2000271 https://doi.org/10.1002/ppsc.202000271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel synthesis of high-thermal stability carbon dots and nanocomposites from oleic acid as an organic substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Rimal, S. Shishodia, P.K. Srivastava, Novel synthesis of high-thermal stability carbon dots and nanocomposites from oleic acid as an organic substrate, Appl. Nanosci. 10 (2020) 455-465 https://doi.org/10.1007/s13204-019-01178-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical properties of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their applicability as fluorescent probes for living cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ni, Q.-Y Li, C.-F. Xu, H.-Q. Lai, Y. Bai, T.-F. Chen, Optical properties of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their applicability as fluorescent probes for living cell imaging, Appl. Surf. Sci. 494 (2019) 377-383 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.07.196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unique approach to debundle carbon nanotubes in polymer matrix using carbon dots for enhanced properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.R. Sreenatha, S. Mandalb, S. Singhb, H. Panigrahia, P. Das, A.K. Bhowmickc, K.D. Kumar, Unique approach to debundle carbon nanotubes in polymer matrix using carbon dots for enhanced properties, Eur. Polym.
